title: Task 4a: cape commit — validate conventional format, reject bulk staging, warn on sensitive files
description:
## Goal
Add cape commit <files...> -m <msg> command that validates conventional commit format, rejects bulk staging, warns on sensitive files, stages named files, and commits.

## Execution mode
AUTO

## Behaviors
- cape commit <files...> -m <msg> stages the named files and creates a git commit
- Validates message matches conventional commit format: type(scope): description or type: description
- Allowed types: feat, fix, refactor, docs, test, chore, ci, perf, build, style, revert
- Rejects bulk staging: . as a file arg is rejected with exit 1
- Warns on sensitive files matching patterns: .env*, *credentials*, *secret*, *.pem, *.key
- Sensitive file warning goes to stderr but commit still proceeds
- Exit code 0 on success, 1 on validation failure
- Returns JSON to stdout: { committed: boolean, message: string, files: string[] } on success
- Returns JSON error to stderr on failure
- CommitService is an Effect service injectable via Layer for testing

## Validation rules
- Message must match: /^(feat|fix|refactor|docs|test|chore|ci|perf|build|style|revert)(\(.+\))?: .+$/
- At least one file must be specified
- No file arg can be . (bulk staging rejection)
- Sensitive file patterns: /^\.env/, /credentials/i, /secret/i, /\.pem$/, /\.key$/

## References
- cli/src/services/git.ts — GitService pattern
- cli/src/services/gitLive.ts — execSync pattern for git commands
- cli/src/commands/check.ts — command wiring pattern with error handling

## Success criteria
- [ ] cape commit file.ts -m "feat: add thing" stages and commits
- [ ] cape commit file.ts -m "bad message" exits 1 with validation error
- [ ] cape commit . -m "feat: bulk" exits 1 with bulk staging rejection
- [ ] cape commit .env -m "feat: add config" warns on stderr but commits
- [ ] cape commit (no files) exits 1 with error
- [ ] Returns structured JSON on success and failure
- [ ] CommitService testable via injected layer
- [ ] Pure validation functions have full test coverage
- [ ] All existing tests still passing
- [ ] vp check passes
close_reason: Implemented in 9174ed1. All success criteria met: conventional format validation, bulk staging rejection, sensitive file warnings, Effect service layer with tests, execFileSync for safe arg passing.

title: Epic: Shared scripts for cape skills and agents
description:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- br-append-design.ts reads existing design field via `br show --format json <id>`, appends a markdown section with `## <heading>`, writes back via `br update <id> --design`
- detect-project.ts detects language, test framework/command, linter, and formatter from CWD
- detect-project.ts supports 5 ecosystems: TypeScript, Go, Lua, Python, Rust
- detect-project.ts uses just/make targets as fallback only when no framework provides the capability
- detect-project.ts --map <dir> maps source files to test files using detected conventions
- run-checks.ts imports lib/detect.ts, runs all detected checks (tests, lint, format), returns structured JSON pass/fail with exit code
- Shared detection logic lives in scripts/lib/detect.ts, imported by detect-project and run-checks
- All scripts use #!/usr/bin/env bun, output JSON to stdout, errors to stderr
- Skills invoke scripts via bun run ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/<name>.ts

## Success criteria
- [ ] br-append-design appends sections to br issues without losing existing content
- [ ] br-append-design handles null design field (first append to empty issue)
- [ ] detect-project correctly identifies language, test framework, linter for each supported ecosystem
- [ ] detect-project --map correctly maps source files to test files
- [ ] detect-project falls back to just/make only when no framework provides the capability
- [ ] run-checks runs all detected checks and returns structured JSON pass/fail
- [ ] Scripts follow validate.ts patterns (Bun APIs, shebang, error handling)
- [ ] All tests passing
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Anti-patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- NO task runner override of framework commands (reason: framework knows its own semantics; just/make only fill gaps where no framework is detected)
- NO monorepo multi-language detection (reason: CWD scoping handles this; skills already operate in specific directories)
- NO caching of detect-project results (reason: YAGNI; re-running is cheap)
- NO separate map-tests CLI script (reason: 2 consumers don't justify a 4th entry point; --map flag suffices)
- NO taskRunner metadata when framework covers the capability (reason: gap-filling only, no redundant info)
- NO bash for new scripts (reason: structured JSON output and config parsing painful in bash; Bun is the established pattern)

## Durable decisions
- Runtime: TypeScript with Bun (#!/usr/bin/env bun), matching validate.ts precedent
- Module structure: scripts/lib/detect.ts shared by detect-project and run-checks; br-append-design standalone
- Detection scope: CWD-based, not project-root-based
- Command preference chain: Framework direct > just <target> > make <target> > absent
- Output shape: Flat JSON — one resolved "best" command per operation
- Deliverable order: br-append-design first (standalone, unblocks 4 skills), detection suite second
- Invocation convention: bun run ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/X.ts in skill bash blocks

## Approach
Build 3 TypeScript (Bun) scripts in scripts/ as shared tooling for cape skills and agents. br-append-design eliminates the error-prone 3-step pattern (br show → extract design → br update --design) repeated across 4 skills. detect-project and run-checks replace ad-hoc project/test detection prose instructions across 6+ skills and agents with structured JSON output.

Scripts follow the established validate.ts pattern: Bun shebang, git root detection, structured output. Skills reference them via explicit ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} paths, matching the hook invocation convention.

## Architecture
scripts/
├── lib/
│   └── detect.ts            # detectProject(), mapTests(), ecosystem configs
├── br-append-design.ts      # standalone: br show --json → append → br update --design
├── detect-project.ts        # CLI wrapper: imports lib, parses args, outputs JSON
├── run-checks.ts            # imports lib, runs detected commands, collects results

Output shapes:
- detect-project: { language, test: { framework, allCommand, fileCommand, filePattern, location }, lint: { command }, format: { command } }
- detect-project --map: { mappings: [{ source, test, exists, skip? }] }
- run-checks: { passed, checks: [{ name, command, passed, exitCode, output? }] }
- br-append-design: { ok, id, section }

## Design rationale

### Problem
Cape skills repeat error-prone shell patterns across 10+ skills and agents. Each re-implements br design appending (4 skills), project/test detection (6+ skills), and check execution (3+ skills) via prose instructions to Claude. This causes inconsistency, fragility, and wasted context tokens on rediscovery.

### Research findings

**Codebase:**
- scripts/validate.ts — Bun shebang, Glob API, git root detection, structured output pattern
- hooks/hooks.json — bun run ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/X.ts invocation pattern
- br show --format json — returns full issue JSON with nullable design field
- br update --design <text> — replaces design field (does not append)
- br schema — emits JSON schemas for all output types
- execute-plan, fix-bug, finish-epic, expand-task — all repeat br show → extract → append → update pattern
- test-runner, TDD, analyze-tests, find-test-gaps, finish-epic, expand-task — all independently discover test framework

### Approaches considered

#### 1. Flat output with task-runner fallback (selected)

**What:** Framework commands resolved first, just/make fill gaps only. Consumers get one command per operation.
**Pros:** Simple for consumers, no decision logic needed, matches how skills actually use the output.
**Cons:** Hides task runner layer entirely when framework is detected.
**Chosen because:** Skills never need both layers simultaneously. Simplicity wins.

#### 2. Layered output (rejected)

**What:** Reports both framework and task runner detection separately. Consumers choose which to use.
**Why explored:** Transparency — consumers see full detection results.
**Why rejected:** Adds decision logic to every consumer for no benefit given task runners are fallback-only.
**DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Task runners need to override framework commands (user explicitly rejected this).

### Scope boundaries

**In scope:** 3 new scripts, 1 shared module, skill updates to reference scripts
**Out of scope:** Agent template standardization (prose-level, not scriptable), new skill creation (pr, review, lint — separate epics), existing skill rewrites beyond updating specific bash blocks

### Open questions
- Exact detection heuristics per ecosystem (discover during implementation)
- How to detect test file location convention (colocated vs tests/ directory) reliably
- Whether run-checks should capture full output or just summary + failures

## Design discovery

### Key decisions made

| Question | Answer | Implication |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| Language for scripts | TypeScript (Bun) | Matches validate.ts, structured output easy |
| Script coupling | Shared module in lib/ | 3 scripts import lib/detect.ts; br-append-design standalone |
| Skill invocation | Explicit ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} path | Zero ambiguity, matches hook convention |
| Ecosystem scope | TS, Go, Lua, Python, Rust + just/make | Detection logic cheap per-ecosystem |
| Task runner priority | Fallback only | Framework > just > make; invisible when framework detected |
| Monorepo strategy | CWD-based detection | Skills already operate in specific directories |
| map-tests | --map flag on detect-project | Function in lib, not separate CLI |
| Deliverables | Two phases | br-append-design first, detection suite second |
| Output format | Flat JSON | One resolved command per operation |

### Dead-end paths

#### Bash scripts
**Why explored:** Shell scripts are natural for CLI wrappers (br, git).
**What found:** Structured JSON output, config file parsing (package.json, Cargo.toml), and error handling are painful in bash.
**Why abandoned:** validate.ts already establishes Bun as the script runtime. Consistency and capability both favor TypeScript.

#### Separate map-tests.ts CLI
**Why explored:** analyze-tests and find-test-gaps both need source→test mapping.
**What found:** Only 2 consumers. The mapping logic is a function call, not a complex pipeline.
**Why abandoned:** --map flag on detect-project covers the need without a 4th CLI entry point.

#### Shipping all scripts as one deliverable
**Why explored:** Cohesive release.
**What found:** br-append-design has zero dependency on detection logic. It's the highest ROI script (4 skills) and simplest to build.
**Why abandoned:** Splitting lets br-append-design ship and unblock skills immediately.

### Open concerns raised
- Monorepo detection? -> CWD scoping handles it naturally
- Task runner vs framework priority? -> Framework wins, user confirmed
- Caching detect-project? -> YAGNI, deferred
close_reason: Superseded by cape-2kp (Cape CLI epic). Shared scripts approach replaced by full CLI.

title: Task 1: Build br-append-design.ts
description:
## Goal
Build scripts/br-append-design.ts — a standalone script that appends a markdown section to a br issue's design field without losing existing content.

## Execution mode
AFK — straightforward implementation following validate.ts patterns.

## Implementation
1. Study existing patterns
   - scripts/validate.ts:1-30 — Bun shebang, git root detection, error handling
   - scripts/beads-sync.sh:1-21 — br CLI interaction pattern, graceful br-not-found handling

2. Write tests first (cape:test-driven-development)
   - Appends section to issue with existing design content
   - Handles null design field (first append to empty issue)
   - Preserves existing content exactly (no truncation)
   - Exits non-zero when issue ID not found
   - Exits non-zero when br is not installed
   - Outputs JSON { ok, id, section } on success

3. Implementation checklist
   - [ ] scripts/br-append-design.ts — parse args (id, heading, content), br show --format json, extract design, append section, br update --design
   - [ ] Graceful exit when br not installed (command -v equivalent)
   - [ ] JSON output to stdout, errors to stderr
   - [ ] #!/usr/bin/env bun shebang

## Interface
Usage: br-append-design <issue-id> <section-heading> <content>
  - Reads via: br show --format json <id>
  - Extracts: design field (nullable string)
  - Appends: \n\n## <heading>\n\n<content>
  - Writes via: br update <id> --design "<combined>"
  - Outputs: { "ok": true, "id": "<id>", "section": "## <heading>" }

## Success criteria
- [ ] Appends to existing design without data loss
- [ ] Handles null design field correctly
- [ ] Returns structured JSON on success
- [ ] Exits non-zero with stderr message on failure
- [ ] Follows validate.ts code patterns
- [ ] Tests passing

title: Task 1: Add cape git diff command
description:
## What
Add `cape git diff [scope]` subcommand that resolves unstaged/staged/branch/PR to the correct git diff flags and outputs diff content.

## Scope values
- `unstaged` (default) — `git diff`
- `staged` — `git diff --cached`
- `branch` — `git diff <main>...HEAD`
- `pr` — same as `branch` but also includes uncommitted changes

## Interface
```
cape git diff [scope]
```
Outputs the raw diff content to stdout. Exits non-zero if not in a git repo.

## Files to create/modify
- `cli/src/services/git.ts` — add `getDiff(scope)` to GitService interface
- `cli/src/services/gitLive.ts` — implement live getDiff
- `cli/src/commands/gitDiff.ts` — new command file
- `cli/src/commands/git.ts` — register diff subcommand
- `cli/src/commands/gitDiff.test.ts` — unit tests
- `cli/src/__e2e__/commands.test.ts` — E2E tests
- `cli/src/testStubs.ts` — update stubGitLayer

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `cape git diff` outputs unstaged diff (default)
- [ ] `cape git diff staged` outputs staged diff
- [ ] `cape git diff branch` outputs diff against main branch
- [ ] `cape git diff pr` outputs combined branch + uncommitted diff
- [ ] Invalid scope argument produces error JSON

title: Task 4: Add cape epic verify command

title: Lightweight TDD guard — soft reminder hooks that inject additionalContext when production files are edited without test evidence
description:
# Lightweight TDD guard (soft reminder)

## Requirements

1. Track test run outcomes (pass/fail) via PostToolUse/PostToolUseFailure hooks on Bash
2. Detect test commands by regex matching on `tool_input.command`
3. After production code file edits, inject `additionalContext` reminders when test evidence is missing or stale (no tests run, or tests all green)
4. Stay silent when: editing test files, editing non-code files, flow-context is not executing/debugging, or tests are red (correct TDD — writing code to pass)
5. Never deny or block — only inject context
6. Clear state on fresh session startup (matching `br-show-log.txt` pattern)
7. Fail open on all errors (JSON parse, br unavailable, missing state)

## Anti-patterns

- No test output parsing — use hook event name for pass/fail
- No per-framework reporter packages
- No project-level artifacts (state lives in hooks/context/, already gitignored)
- No blocking or denying edits

## Success criteria

1. bun test passes for all new test files
2. Production file edit during executing phase with no prior test run → reminder injected
3. Production file edit during executing phase after failing test → no reminder
4. Production file edit during executing phase after passing test → reminder injected
5. Test file edit → no reminder regardless of state
6. Non-code file edit → no reminder regardless of state
7. Edit during idle/planning phase → no reminder regardless of state
8. Corrupted or missing state → no crash, no reminder (fail open)

title: Shared module hooks/tdd.ts — file classification, test command detection, state read/write
description:
## Goal

Create the shared module hooks/tdd.ts with file classification, test command detection, and TDD state read/write.

## Behaviors

1. isTestFile returns true for .test.ts, .spec.tsx, _test.go, _spec.lua, test_foo.py, __tests__/foo.ts — and false for foo.ts, test-utils.ts, foo.py
2. isCodeFile returns true for .ts, .go, .py, .rs, .lua etc — and false for .json, .md, .yaml, .css, .html
3. isTestCommand returns true for bun test, npm test, vitest, pytest, go test, cargo test, etc — and false for bun run build, echo test, git status
4. writeTddState creates hooks/context/tdd-state.json with phase and timestamp; readTddState reads it back; returns null when file missing or corrupted

## Success criteria

- All four behaviors tested and passing in hooks/tdd.test.ts
- State file created in contextDir (uses tddState from paths.ts)
- paths.ts exports tddState pointing to hooks/context/tdd-state.json

title: PostToolUse hooks — track test outcomes and remind on production edits
description:
## Goal

Create two hooks:
1. **track-test-outcome.ts** (PostToolUse + PostToolUseFailure on Bash) — detects test commands via `isTestCommand`, writes TDD state with `writeTddState`
2. **tdd-reminder.ts** (PostToolUse on Edit) — after production code edits, injects `additionalContext` reminder when test evidence is missing or stale

## Behaviors

### track-test-outcome.ts
1. On PostToolUse/Bash: if `isTestCommand(tool_input.command)` → `writeTddState({ phase: "green", timestamp })` 
2. On PostToolUseFailure/Bash: if `isTestCommand(tool_input.command)` → `writeTddState({ phase: "red", timestamp })`
3. Non-test commands → exit 0, no state change
4. Invalid JSON input → exit 0 (fail open)

### tdd-reminder.ts
1. Read `tool_input.file_path` from stdin
2. If `isTestFile(file_path)` → exit 0 (no reminder)
3. If not `isCodeFile(file_path)` → exit 0 (no reminder)
4. Read flow-context from `deriveFlowContext` — if phase is not executing/debugging → exit 0
5. Read TDD state via `readTddState` — if phase is "red" → exit 0 (correct TDD flow)
6. If phase is "green" or state is null → output `additionalContext` reminder
7. Any error → exit 0 (fail open)

## Success criteria

- track-test-outcome: test command on PostToolUse → state written as green
- track-test-outcome: test command on PostToolUseFailure → state written as red
- track-test-outcome: non-test command → no state change
- tdd-reminder: production file edit with no test state → reminder injected
- tdd-reminder: production file edit with green state → reminder injected
- tdd-reminder: production file edit with red state → no reminder
- tdd-reminder: test file edit → no reminder
- tdd-reminder: non-code file edit → no reminder
- tdd-reminder: edit during idle/planning phase → no reminder
- tdd-reminder: corrupted state → no crash, no reminder
- hooks.json updated with both new hooks
- session-start.ts clears tdd-state.json on startup (matching br-show-log.txt pattern)

title: Epic: Cape CLI — unified CLI for deterministic operations and hook dispatch
description:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- cape binary available in PATH via pnpm global install or symlink
- Runtime: Node (no bun dependency), bundled via Vite+ toolchain (tsdown), #!/usr/bin/env node shebang
- Built with Effect v4 (effect/unstable/cli, @effect/platform-node)

## Success criteria
- [ ] cape --help lists all subcommands (cape hook hidden from output)
- [ ] cape detect correctly identifies language, test framework, linter for 5 ecosystems

- [ ] cape hook pre-tool-use --matcher Skill gates skill loading correctly

- [ ] cape hook post-tool-use-failure tracks test failures with TDD_PHASE=red
- [ ] Protocol envelope shape tests assert exact JSON keys per output type (additionalContext, decision+additionalContext, hookSpecificOutput)
- [ ] Pass-through response is empty stdout + exit 0 (never an "allow" JSON)
- [ ] E2E flow test: br show then br update --design succeeds (track-br-show writes log, enforce-commands reads it)
- [ ] E2E flow test: PR confirmation then PR creation succeeds within TTL, fails after expiry (track-pr-confirmation writes, enforce-commands reads/deletes)

- [ ] All existing hook test cases pass against new CLI implementations
- [ ] track-pr-confirmation has full test suite before migration

- [ ] Old hooks removed, hooks.json points at cape hook commands
- [ ] All tests passing
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing
- [ ] Skill updates documented as follow-up (6+ skills reference prose that CLI now enforces)

## Anti-patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- NO intent classification in CLI (reason: routing is an LLM task)
- NO wrapping br for CRUD operations (reason: br works fine as-is)
- NO commit grouping logic (reason: grouping requires LLM judgment)
- NO workflow state machine (reason: chain transitions need conversational context)
- NO bun dependency (reason: Node is universal)
- NO assuming uniform hook protocol (reason: 5 input shapes, 3 output shapes)
- NO dropping enforcement guards (reason: Claude may bypass after context compression)
- NO tsup for bundling (reason: unmaintained; use Vite+ tsdown)
- NO hook entry points that exit non-zero on errors (reason: hooks must fail-open with exit 0; use Effect.catchAllDefect wrapping distinct from CLI command error handling)
- NO hook logic modules inside cli/src/ (reason: hooks have a different runtime model — stdin/stdout protocol, always exit 0 — than CLI commands which use non-zero exits)

## Durable decisions
- Runtime: Node + Effect v4 + Vite+ toolchain (no bun)
- Package manager: pnpm
- CLI framework: effect/unstable/cli with Command.make and withSubcommands
- Platform services: @effect/platform-node
- Binary name: cape, in PATH
- Hook dispatcher: receives event type + matcher as CLI args, routes internally
- Hook event names: accept both PascalCase (PreToolUse) and kebab-case (pre-tool-use)
- Hook session-start: --clear-logs flag replaces CAPE_CLEAR_LOGS=1 env var
- Hook entry points: always exit 0 via Effect.catchAllDefect wrapping (fail-open)
- Hook logic placement: repo root /hooks/, not inside cli/src/ (different runtime model)
- Dual role: validated commands AND bypass guards in same binary
- Br interaction: validate + design commands only
- Output: JSON to stdout, errors to stderr (human-readable on TTY via isTTY detection)
- Exit codes: 0 success, 1 validation/domain failure, 2 usage error
- Protocol module: protocol.ts owns all input/output type definitions
- Cutover: hard, with migrated + new test coverage
- Development: strict TDD
- E2E flow tests: spawn cape hook commands in sequence with real temp git repos and context/ dirs, verifying cross-hook state propagation
- Reference: greppa/main patterns
- Task count: 10 tasks (T1 scaffolding, T2a detect, T2b detect --map, T3 check, T4a commit, T4b br, T5 pr, T6a hooks session/prompt, T6b hooks pre-tool-use, T6c hooks post-tool-use, T7 cutover)
- Post-cutover follow-up: update 6+ skills to reference CLI commands instead of prose enforcement

## Approach
Build a cape CLI as a single pnpm package under cli/. Effect services provide testable domain logic. Commands are thin wiring via effect/unstable/cli. Hook logic stays at repo root /hooks/ with shared pure functions importable from cli/. A protocol module defines all hook input/output shapes as the single source of truth.

## Architecture
cli/src/
  index.ts             — entry: NodeServices.layer + NodeRuntime.runMain
  main.ts              — Command.make('cape') + withSubcommands
  commands/            — 9 modules (detect, check, git, commit, pr, br, hook, validate, context-internal)
  services/            — 8 Effect services (Git, Br, Detect, Check, BrValidate, GitGuard, Tdd, Context, Project)
  protocol.ts          — 5 input interfaces, 3 output constructors (denyWith, approveWith, addContext)
hooks/                 — 9 hook logic modules (repo root, not inside cli/src/)

Services:
- Git: spawn git, detect branch, check status
- Br: spawn br, query flow state, derive phase
- Project: detect repo root via .git/.beads walk-up, resolve plugin root
- Context: read/write all cross-hook state files (tdd-state.json, br-show-log.txt, pr-confirmed.txt)
- BrValidate: br create/update rule enforcement (rules 1-6, 13 from enforce-commands)
- GitGuard: git staging and PR creation guards (rules 7-12 from enforce-commands)
- Check: run detected tests, lint, format — returns structured pass/fail
- Tdd: test-file pattern detection, phase tracking, state read/write

Cross-hook state files (managed by Context service):
- hooks/context/tdd-state.json — written by track-test-outcome, read by tdd-reminder, cleared by session-start
- hooks/context/br-show-log.txt — written by track-br-show, read by enforce-commands rule 2, cleared by session-start
- hooks/context/pr-confirmed.txt — written/deleted by track-pr-confirmation, read/deleted by enforce-commands rule 12
design:
## E2E flow tests (2026-03-27)

Unit tests verify each hook in isolation. Protocol tests verify output shapes. E2E flow tests verify the cross-hook state chains that make the system work as a whole.

### Why unit tests are insufficient for hooks
The hook system has 3 cross-boundary state chains where one hook writes a file and a different hook reads it later. A unit test can pass for both sides independently while missing: wrong file path, wrong JSON shape, wrong timestamp format, stale state from a prior test. These are integration-level bugs that only surface when hooks run in sequence against shared state.

### Flow test design
Each flow test creates a real temp git repo with a real context/ directory. It spawns cape hook commands in sequence, simulating the event order Claude Code produces. Assertions verify the final observable outcome, not intermediate state.

**Flow 1: br show then br update --design**
1. Spawn `cape hook post-tool-use --matcher Bash` with stdin `{ tool_input: { command: "br show cape-abc" } }`
2. Assert br-show-log.txt contains "cape-abc"
3. Spawn `cape hook pre-tool-use --matcher Bash` with stdin `{ tool_input: { command: "br update cape-abc --design '## New'" } }`
4. Assert exit 0 + empty stdout (allowed)
5. Without step 1, assert step 3 produces deny

**Flow 2: PR confirmation gate with TTL**
1. Spawn `cape hook post-tool-use --matcher AskUserQuestion` with stdin containing a PR-related question and confirming answer
2. Assert pr-confirmed.txt exists with a recent timestamp
3. Spawn `cape hook pre-tool-use --matcher Bash` with stdin containing a PR creation command
4. Assert exit 0 + empty stdout (allowed)
5. Overwrite pr-confirmed.txt with an 11-minute-old timestamp
6. Repeat step 3, assert deny with expiry reason

**Flow 3: TDD red-green-refactor cycle**
1. Spawn `cape hook post-tool-use-failure --matcher Bash` with stdin containing a test command (TDD_PHASE=red env)
2. Assert tdd-state.json has phase "red"
3. Spawn `cape hook post-tool-use --matcher Edit` with stdin containing a production file path
4. Assert additionalContext contains TDD reminder
5. Spawn `cape hook post-tool-use --matcher Bash` with stdin containing a test command (phase defaults to green)
6. Assert tdd-state.json has phase "green"
7. Repeat step 3, assert no TDD reminder (fresh green state)

**Flow 4: Session start clears stale state**
1. Write stale data to br-show-log.txt, tdd-state.json, pr-confirmed.txt
2. Spawn `cape hook session-start --clear-logs`
3. Assert all three files are cleared/deleted
4. Subsequent hook spawns start with clean state

**Flow 5: Full commit pipeline**
1. In temp git repo with staged changes
2. Run `cape commit file.ts -m "feat: add thing"`
3. Assert exit 0, git log shows the commit
4. Run `cape commit file.ts -m "bad message"`
5. Assert exit 1, no commit created
6. Run `cape commit . -m "feat: bulk"`
7. Assert exit 1, bulk staging rejected

### Test infrastructure
- Temp git repo per test (git init + initial commit)
- Real context/ directory under temp root
- CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT pointed at temp root
- Subprocess spawning via Node child_process (not Effect layers — these are binary-level tests)
- Cleanup via afterEach rm -rf

### Where these live
`cli/src/__e2e__/` or `cli/src/flows.test.ts` — separate from unit tests, can be tagged for slower CI runs if needed. Run as part of the migration acceptance gate before cutover.

title: Task 2a: cape detect command — ecosystem detection from CWD
description:
## Goal
Implement `cape detect` that inspects the current working directory and outputs JSON describing the detected ecosystem: language, test framework, linter, and formatter.

## Execution mode
AUTO (patterns established in T1, no ambiguity)

## Behaviors
- cape detect outputs JSON with `language` field (typescript, go, lua, python, rust)
- cape detect outputs JSON with `testFramework` field (vitest, jest, go-test, busted, pytest, cargo-test, null)
- cape detect outputs JSON with `linter` field (oxlint, eslint, golangci-lint, luacheck, ruff, clippy, null)
- cape detect outputs JSON with `formatter` field (oxfmt, prettier, gofmt, stylua, ruff, rustfmt, null)
- Detection uses file presence heuristics (package.json, go.mod, .stylua.toml, pyproject.toml, Cargo.toml)
- When multiple signals conflict, language with strongest signal wins
- cape detect returns exit code 1 with error JSON when no ecosystem detected
- DetectService is an Effect service injectable via Layer for testing
- cape --help lists detect subcommand

## Detection heuristics (per ecosystem)
- **TypeScript**: package.json with typescript dep or tsconfig.json
  - vitest: vitest in devDeps or vite.config with test
  - jest: jest in devDeps
  - oxlint: .oxlintrc.json or oxlint in devDeps
  - eslint: .eslintrc* or eslint in devDeps
  - oxfmt: .oxfmtrc.json or oxfmt in devDeps
  - prettier: .prettierrc* or prettier in devDeps
- **Go**: go.mod present
  - go-test (built-in), golangci-lint (.golangci.yml), gofmt (built-in)
- **Lua**: .stylua.toml or .luacheckrc or init.lua
  - busted: .busted or spec/ directory, luacheck: .luacheckrc, stylua: .stylua.toml
- **Python**: pyproject.toml or setup.py or requirements.txt
  - pytest: pytest in deps, ruff: ruff in deps or ruff.toml
- **Rust**: Cargo.toml present
  - cargo-test (built-in), clippy (built-in), rustfmt (built-in)

## References
- cli/src/services/git.ts — Effect service pattern from T1
- cli/src/services/gitLive.ts — live layer pattern from T1

title: Task 6b: cape hook pre-tool-use — Bash and Skill matchers
description:
## Goal
Add pre-tool-use event handling to the cape hook dispatcher. Migrates enforce-commands (Bash matcher) and flow-gate (Skill matcher) logic into the CLI HookService as pure, testable functions.

## Execution mode
AUTO

## Behaviors
### cape hook pre-tool-use --matcher Bash
- Reads command from stdin JSON (tool_input.command)
- Applies all 13 enforcement guards from hooks/enforce-commands.ts:
  1. --design on br create → deny (use --description)
  2. br update --design without prior br show → deny (reads br-show-log.txt)
  3. br create missing --type or --priority → deny
  4. br create description header validation per type (task needs Goal/Behaviors/Success criteria, bug needs Reproduction steps or Evidence, epic needs Requirements/Success criteria)
  5. --status in-progress (hyphen) or --status done on br update → deny
  6. Bulk git staging (dot or -A/--all) → deny
  7. PR creation from default branch → deny
  8. PR creation with uncommitted changes → deny
  9. PR creation --body with invented sections → deny
  10. PR creation without pr-confirmed.txt or expired TTL → deny (consumes confirmation)
  11. br create missing --labels → deny
  12. stop-reinforcement: br close command → output additionalContext with stop message
- Accumulates violations and denies with combined reason
- Git commands (branch check, status check) use HookService.spawnGit
- Pass-through: empty stdout + exit 0

### cape hook pre-tool-use --matcher Skill
- Reads skill name from stdin JSON (tool_input.skill)
- Gates execute-plan: requires open epic + ready tasks
- Gates finish-epic: requires all tasks under epic closed
- Gates fix-bug: requires open bug
- Uses HookService.brQuery for br checks
- Non-gated skills pass through
- br unavailability → allow through (fail-open)

### Protocol output
- Deny: { hookSpecificOutput: { hookEventName: "PreToolUse", permissionDecision: "deny", permissionDecisionReason: string } }
- Approve with context: { additionalContext: string } (for stop-reinforcement on br close)
- Pass-through: empty stdout

### cape hook post-tool-use --matcher Bash
- Reads command from stdin JSON (tool_input.command)
- Detects br show commands via regex, appends bead ID to br-show-log.txt
- Detects test commands (bun test, npm test, vitest, pytest, go test, cargo test, busted, python -m pytest/unittest), writes tdd-state.json with phase green
- Side-effect only: no stdout output, exit 0
- Creates context dir if missing
- Invalid/empty input: exit 0 silently

### cape hook post-tool-use --matcher Edit
- Reads file path from stdin JSON (tool_input.file_path)
- Skips test files (.test.ts, _test.go, _spec.lua, test_*.py, __tests__/)
- Skips non-code files (only .ts/.tsx/.js/.jsx/.go/.py/.rs/.lua are code files)

- Reads tdd-state.json — skips if fresh red state (< 10 min TTL)

### cape hook post-tool-use --matcher AskUserQuestion
- Reads questions/answers from stdin JSON (tool_input.questions[], tool_input.answers{})
- Checks if any question text matches PR keywords (/\bpr\b|pull request/i)

- Confirmed: writes Date.now() to pr-confirmed.txt
- Rejected (cancel/abort/edit/revise): deletes pr-confirmed.txt
- Creates context dir if missing

### cape hook post-tool-use-failure --matcher Bash
- Reads command from stdin JSON (tool_input.command)
- Detects test commands (same pattern as PostToolUse Bash)
- Writes tdd-state.json with phase red (distinct from green in PostToolUse)

title: Epic: Hook-based workflow flow enforcement
description:
## Requirements
- PreToolUse hook on Skill tool denies `execute-plan` when no open epic or no ready tasks exist (br state check)
- PreToolUse hook on Skill tool denies `finish-epic` when open tasks remain under the epic (br state check)
- PreToolUse hook on Skill tool denies `fix-bug` when no bug exists in br (br state check)
- Only three skills are phase-gated: execute-plan, finish-epic, fix-bug. All other skills pass through ungated
- UserPromptSubmit hook injects chain-position context ("you are in phase X") on every user message, derived from br state
- SessionStart hook injects phase context on startup, resume, clear, and compact events
- PostToolUse hook on Bash detects `br close` commands and injects additionalContext telling the LLM to stop — no tool denial
- Deny messages include the correct skill to load instead
- br is the single source of truth — no separate state files or CLI tools
- br unavailability defaults to permissive (never deny based on absence)
- Existing hook behavior preserved (TDD injection, edit tracking, command enforcement, gentle reminders)

## Durable decisions
- Gate checks use br CLI directly via Bun.spawnSync, same pattern as existing user-prompt-submit.ts TDD check
- Deny response shape matches enforce-commands.ts: { hookSpecificOutput: { hookEventName, permissionDecision, permissionDecisionReason } }
- No output = allow, matching existing hook convention
- Task closure detection regex targets `br close` (not `br update --status closed`)
- Chain-position injection wrapped in `<flow-context>` tags for distinguishability
- Brainstorm remains side-effect-free; brainstorm→write-plan stays soft-enforced via conversation context

## Anti-patterns
- NO separate state files (reason: creates two sources of truth that drift from br)
- NO blanket tool denial / pausePending mechanism (reason: deadlocks skills that need tools to finish gracefully)
- NO PostToolUse/Skill for phase advancement (reason: fires at skill LOAD not COMPLETION, would deadlock every skill invocation)
- NO gating of read-only tools (reason: blocking them creates deadlocks for no benefit)
- NO gating of write-plan or brainstorm (reason: write-plan creates epics, gating it blocks multi-epic workflows; brainstorm is side-effect-free by design)
- NO cape-flow CLI tool (reason: br is already the state machine; parallel CLI adds unnecessary concepts)
- NO restructuring brainstorm to produce br artifacts (reason: enforcement should serve the workflow, not the other way around)

## Success criteria
- [ ] execute-plan denied when no open epic exists
- [ ] execute-plan denied when no ready tasks exist
- [ ] finish-epic denied when open tasks remain under the epic
- [ ] fix-bug denied when no bug exists in br
- [ ] Non-gated skills (commit, review, beads, branch, brainstorm, write-plan) always allowed
- [ ] Deny messages include the correct skill to load instead
- [ ] Chain-position context injected on every user message via UserPromptSubmit
- [ ] Phase context injected on session start/resume/clear/compact via SessionStart
- [ ] br close in Bash triggers stop-reinforcement additionalContext
- [ ] br unavailability defaults to allow (no crashes)
- [ ] Existing hook behavior preserved
- [ ] Tests passing
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

**Summary:** Built three-layer flow enforcement via Claude Code hooks: (1) flow-gate.ts PreToolUse/Skill hook denies execute-plan, finish-epic, and fix-bug when br prerequisites are unmet; (2) flow-context injection in UserPromptSubmit and SessionStart hooks tells the LLM its current phase (idle/planning/executing/debugging); (3) stop-reinforcement.ts PostToolUse/Bash hook injects additionalContext on br close to halt forward progress. All layers default to permissive when br is unavailable. No separate state files — br is the single source of truth.
**Verification:** All 109 hook tests passing, all 13 success criteria met with evidence, pre-commit hooks clean.

## Goal
Create the PreToolUse/Skill hook that denies phase-gated skill invocations when br state prerequisites are not met. This is the core enforcement mechanism — the hardest gate in the system.

title: Task 3: PostToolUse/Bash hook for br close stop-reinforcement
description:
## Goal
Add PostToolUse/Bash hook logic that detects `br close` commands and injects additionalContext telling the LLM to stop working and present a checkpoint.

## Context
Tasks 1 and 2 built the hard gate (flow-gate.ts) and soft injection (flow-context in user-prompt-submit.ts and session-start.ts). This task adds the third enforcement layer: stop-reinforcement when a task is closed via `br close`.

## Execution mode
HITL

## Behaviors
- PostToolUse/Bash hook detects `br close` in the command string
- Detection regex targets `br close` (not `br update --status closed`) per epic durable decisions
- Injects additionalContext reinforcing that the LLM should stop, present a checkpoint, and wait for user input
- Does NOT deny the tool — this is soft reinforcement, not a gate
- Existing PostToolUse/Bash behavior preserved (track-br-show, track-edits)
- No output when command doesn't match (existing convention)

## References
- hooks/track-br-show.ts (existing PostToolUse/Bash hook — stdin parsing, command regex pattern)
- hooks/hooks.json (PostToolUse/Bash registration)

design:
## Goal
Add PostToolUse/Bash hook logic that detects `br close` commands and injects additionalContext telling the LLM to stop working and present a checkpoint.

## Context
Tasks 1 and 2 built the hard gate (flow-gate.ts) and soft injection (flow-context in user-prompt-submit.ts and session-start.ts). This task adds the third enforcement layer: stop-reinforcement when a task is closed via `br close`.

## Execution mode
HITL

## Behaviors
- PostToolUse/Bash hook detects `br close` in the command string
- Detection regex targets `br close` (not `br update --status closed`) per epic durable decisions
- Injects additionalContext reinforcing that the LLM should stop, present a checkpoint, and wait for user input
- Does NOT deny the tool — this is soft reinforcement, not a gate
- Existing PostToolUse/Bash behavior preserved (track-br-show, track-edits)
- No output when command doesn't match (existing convention)

## References
- hooks/track-br-show.ts (existing PostToolUse/Bash hook — stdin parsing, command regex pattern)
- hooks/hooks.json (PostToolUse/Bash registration)
- hooks/flow-gate.ts:22-33 (deny response shape — NOT used here, but for reference on what NOT to do)

## Success criteria
- [x] `br close` in Bash command triggers stop-reinforcement additionalContext
- [x] Non-close commands produce no output
- [x] Existing PostToolUse/Bash behavior preserved
- [x] Tests passing
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Expanded plan (expand-task)

### Investigation findings

**Files to modify:**
- `hooks/hooks.json:65-72` — add stop-reinforcement.ts to PostToolUse/Bash hooks array
- `hooks/stop-reinforcement.ts` (new) — PostToolUse/Bash hook detecting `br close`
- `hooks/stop-reinforcement.test.ts` (new) — tests

**Patterns to follow:**
- `hooks/track-br-show.ts:1-21` — exact PostToolUse/Bash stdin parsing + command regex pattern
- `hooks/session-start.ts:74` — `additionalContext` JSON output shape: `{ additionalContext: "..." }`
- `hooks/track-br-show.test.ts:1-84` — test structure: tmpDir setup, `runHook()` helper, describe/it blocks

**Reusable code:**
- No shared utilities — each hook inlines stdin parsing (convention)

**Blast radius:**
- `hooks/hooks.json` — adding entry to existing PostToolUse/Bash hooks array; existing hooks unaffected
- No callers/importers — hook is invoked by Claude Code harness via hooks.json

### Steps

#### Step 1: Create stop-reinforcement.ts hook

**Pattern:** `hooks/track-br-show.ts:1-21` — stdin parse + command regex

**Changes:**
- `hooks/stop-reinforcement.ts` (new) — PostToolUse/Bash hook following track-br-show pattern:
  1. Read stdin via `Bun.stdin.text()`
  2. Parse JSON, extract `tool_input.command`
  3. Regex match `/\bbr\s+close\b/` on command
  4. If match: `console.log(JSON.stringify({ additionalContext: "..." }))` with stop-reinforcement message
  5. If no match: `process.exit(0)` (silent, no output)

**Test:** Test that `br close <id>` produces additionalContext JSON, non-close commands produce no output, invalid JSON exits silently.

**Verify:** `bun test hooks/stop-reinforcement.test.ts`

#### Step 2: Register in hooks.json

**Pattern:** `hooks/hooks.json:65-72` — existing PostToolUse/Bash entry

**Changes:**
- `hooks/hooks.json:66-71` — add stop-reinforcement.ts as second entry in the Bash matcher's `hooks` array

**Test:** Existing hooks continue to work; new hook registered.

**Verify:** `bun test hooks/` (run all hook tests)

### Final verification
`bun test hooks/`
`git diff --stat`

## Outcome
Created `hooks/stop-reinforcement.ts` — a PostToolUse/Bash hook that detects `br close` commands and injects additionalContext telling the LLM to stop and present a checkpoint. Registered in hooks.json alongside track-br-show.ts. 7 tests covering: br close with/without args, non-br commands, br show, br update --status closed, empty command, invalid JSON. All 109 hook tests passing.

title: Enhance brainstorm skill with grill-me and parallel design agents
description:
## Goal
Enhance the brainstorm skill with two changes inspired by Matt Pocock's skills repo.

## Changes

### 1. Step 2 — Parallel constraint-divergent sub-agents for approach proposals
Replace inline "propose 2-3 approaches" with dispatching sub-agents that each design under a different constraint:
- Agent 1: "Minimize the interface — simplest possible approach"
- Agent 2: "Maximize flexibility — support many use cases"
- Agent 3: "Optimize for the most common case"

Each returns: approach description, pros/cons, trade-offs. Lead agent compares and recommends.

Inspired by: `design-an-interface` from mattpocock/skills.

### 2. Step 3 — Grill-me cadence for assumption challenge
Replace the lightweight assumption challenge with a deeper grill-me interrogation:
- One question per turn, with recommended answer
- Walk every branch of the decision tree
- Explore codebase to answer own questions when possible
- Continue until shared understanding (all branches resolved)

Inspired by: `grill-me` from mattpocock/skills.

## Success criteria
- [ ] Step 2 dispatches parallel sub-agents with conflicting constraints
- [ ] Step 3 uses grill-me cadence (one question, recommended answer, wait)
- [ ] Brainstorm still produces structured design summary as handoff to write-plan
- [ ] No regression in brainstorm's existing research and decision-tracking capabilities

## Reference
- Matt Pocock's skills: https://github.com/mattpocock/skills
- `grill-me`: relentless 1-question-at-a-time interview until every decision branch resolved
- `design-an-interface`: 3+ parallel sub-agents with conflicting design constraints

title: Skill: design-an-interface
description:
## Goal
Create a skill that generates multiple radically different interface designs using parallel sub-agents with conflicting constraints.

## Process
1. Gather requirements — problem solved, intended callers, key operations, constraints, what to hide vs expose
2. Spawn 3+ parallel sub-agents, each designing under a different constraint:
   - "Minimize method count — 1-3 methods max"
   - "Maximize flexibility — support many use cases"
   - "Optimize for the most common case"
   - "Ports & adapters pattern" (if applicable)
3. Each agent returns: interface signature, usage examples, hidden complexity, trade-offs
4. Compare designs across: simplicity, general-purpose vs specialized, implementation efficiency, depth (small interface hiding complexity = good)
5. Recommend strongest design, propose hybrids if appropriate

## Inspiration
- `design-an-interface` from mattpocock/skills
- "Design It Twice" from John Ousterhout's "A Philosophy of Software Design"
- Deep modules: small interface hiding large implementation = good; large interface with thin implementation = bad

## Success criteria
- [ ] Spawns 3+ parallel sub-agents with different constraint briefs
- [ ] Each agent produces interface signature, usage examples, trade-offs
- [ ] Compares and recommends with rationale
- [ ] Works standalone or as a step within brainstorm

title: Skill: ubiquitous-language
description:
## Goal
Create a skill that extracts DDD-style domain glossaries from conversations and formalizes them into a structured UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md file.

## Process
1. Scan conversation for domain concepts
2. Identify problems: ambiguity, synonyms, vagueness
3. Propose canonical terms (be opinionated)
4. Write UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md with:
   - Tables grouped by domain cluster (e.g., lifecycle, actors, relationships)
   - Three columns: Term, Definition (one sentence max), Aliases to avoid
   - Relationships section showing cardinality between terms
   - Example dialogue (3-5 exchanges) demonstrating correct usage
   - Flagged ambiguities with recommendations
5. On re-run: read existing file, merge new terms, mark updates

## Post-output
State: "I've written/updated UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md. From this point forward I will use these terms consistently."

## Inspiration
- `ubiquitous-language` from mattpocock/skills
- Domain-Driven Design (Eric Evans)

## Success criteria
- [ ] Extracts domain terms from conversation context
- [ ] Groups by domain cluster with clear definitions
- [ ] Flags ambiguities and synonyms
- [ ] Merges gracefully on re-run (marks new/updated entries)
- [ ] Commits to using terms consistently after creation

title: Investigate don-cape vs using-hyper differences
description: Compare cape:don-cape and hyperpowers:using-hyper meta-skills. Determine if they differ in routing, enforcement, or skill chain behavior. Decide which approach is more robust and whether they should converge.
close_reason: Investigated. Don-cape sufficient for cape's scope. No convergence needed.

comments.author: Oskar Grunning
comments.text:
## Findings: don-cape vs using-hyper

Investigated by spawning 3 parallel judge agents (workflow, quality, meta) that read all 34 skill files.

### Routing mechanism

- **don-cape:** 10-route routing table mapping user intents to skills. Checks table, dispatches if match found.
- **using-hyper:** "1% chance" rule — before ANY response, ask "does any skill match?" Even if no obvious table match, forces consideration.

**Winner:** using-hyper is more bulletproof for edge cases (e.g., "what's the best way to approach X?" might not match don-cape's table but triggers brainstorm under using-hyper's 1% rule). However, don-cape's explicit table is simpler and sufficient if the 10 routes cover all cape skills — which they do.

### Enforcement

- **don-cape:** "If a skill matches the task, using it is mandatory." Hard stops between phases (brainstorm STOP, write-plan STOP, each task STOP).
- **using-hyper:** "Finding a relevant skill = mandatory to use it." Less explicit about inter-phase stops; assumes continuation.

**Winner:** Cape's hard stops are genuinely better — they prevent momentum from overriding judgment.

### Skill chain behavior

- **don-cape:** Enforces chains: brainstorm → write-plan → execute-plan, debug → fix-bug. Explicit in routing table.
- **using-hyper:** Enforces chains via "mandatory workflows" section but with more skills in the chain (e.g., sre-task-refinement gate in execute-plan, verification-before-completion before claiming done).

**Winner:** Tie. Cape chains are simpler with fewer gates. Hyperpowers chains are more thorough but add overhead.

### Verdict

Philosophically aligned but differ in scope. Don-cape is sufficient for cape's 12-skill system. Using-hyper needs its broader matching because hyperpowers has 22+ skills where table-based routing would be unwieldy. No convergence needed — each fits its system.

### Gaps identified (tracked elsewhere)

- Cape's biggest gap vs hyperpowers: no verification gate before completion (planned as finish-epic cape-2vo.8)
- No test quality protection (planned as find-test-gaps cape-2vo.7 and analyze-tests cape-2vo.21)
- No post-implementation code review (planned as review cape-2vo.11)

title: Epic: Hallucination reduction for research agents
description:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- internet-researcher cites sources inline (URL + source tier) for every factual claim
- internet-researcher extracts word-for-word quotes from documents before analyzing them
- internet-researcher states "I don't have enough information" rather than speculating when evidence is insufficient
- codebase-investigator cites file:line evidence for every claim about the codebase
- New fact-checker agent audits claims against evidence, retracting unsupported ones
- brainstorm dispatches fact-checker on the design summary before presenting it (step 3)

## Success criteria
- [ ] internet-researcher output includes inline citations (URL + tier) for every factual claim
- [ ] internet-researcher extracts direct quotes before analysis when processing documents
- [ ] internet-researcher explicitly states uncertainty rather than speculating
- [ ] codebase-investigator output includes file:line references for every claim
- [ ] fact-checker agent exists and can verify/retract claims against evidence
- [ ] brainstorm step 3 dispatches fact-checker on design summary before presenting to user
- [ ] All existing agent functionality preserved (no regressions)
- [ ] bun scripts/validate.ts passes

## Anti-patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- NO speculative claims without sources (reason: defeats hallucination reduction)
- NO fact-checker on raw research findings (reason: slows brainstorm, duplicates researcher's citation work)
- NO persistent mode toggle or hook state (reason: fragile state management, against Cape's architecture)
- NO changes to main conversation behavior (reason: creative output tradeoff — guard at agent layer)
- NO fact-checker in execute-plan (reason: user explicitly scoped to brainstorm only)

## Durable decisions
- Citation format: inline (URL + tier), not summary sections — citations travel with claims through skill chains
- Fact-checker scope: brainstorm step 3 only, not execute-plan or other skills
- Agent model: haiku for fact-checker (consistent with existing research agents)
- Guards live at the agent layer, not main conversation — preserves creative output for design thinking
- Fact-checker runs on the design summary (once at the end), not on every research finding

## Approach
Enhance the two existing research agents (internet-researcher, codebase-investigator) with Anthropic's three hallucination reduction techniques: explicit uncertainty admission, citation requirements, and direct quote extraction. These are additive changes to agent instruction files — existing behavior preserved, new requirements layered on top.

Add a new fact-checker agent that takes claims from a design summary, searches for supporting evidence, and retracts any claims it cannot verify. Brainstorm dispatches this agent at step 3 (before presenting the design summary) as a final verification pass.

The creative/research tradeoff resolves naturally: agents do rigorous cited research, main conversation stays free for design thinking.

## Architecture
- agents/internet-researcher.md — add citation protocol (inline URL + tier), quote extraction requirement, explicit "I don't have enough information" protocol
- agents/codebase-investigator.md — strengthen file:line evidence requirement (partially exists)
- agents/fact-checker.md (new) — claim verification agent with source tier awareness
- skills/brainstorm/SKILL.md — add fact-checker dispatch at step 3, after assumption challenge, before presenting design summary

## Design rationale

### Problem
Research done by Cape agents and brainstorm can produce unsourced or hallucinated claims that flow unchecked into designs and plans. The internet-researcher already has source tiers and uncertainty handling, but doesn't enforce citation on every claim or require quote extraction.

### Research findings

**Codebase:**
- agents/internet-researcher.md — already has source tiers table and "Handle uncertainty" section; foundation to build on
- agents/codebase-investigator.md — already has "Verify, don't assume" and file:line reporting; needs minor strengthening
- skills/brainstorm/SKILL.md — step 3 already has assumption challenge; fact-checker dispatch fits after this

**External:**
- Anthropic docs (code.claude.com) — three core techniques: "I don't know" permission, direct quote extraction, cite-or-retract
- arXiv 2307.02185 — citation constraints reduce creative output, validating "guard at agent layer" decision

### Approaches considered

#### 1. Enhance agents + fact-checker (selected)

**What:** Add hallucination reduction techniques to existing research agents; create fact-checker agent dispatched by brainstorm.
**Pros:** Guards activate automatically whenever research happens, composable, no state management, creative output preserved in main conversation.
**Cons:** Only protects agent-mediated research, not main conversation directly.
**Chosen because:** Solves the problem at the right layer — research agents are where factual claims originate.

#### 2. New research skill (rejected)

**What:** Create a dedicated /cape:research skill as a research workflow.
**Why explored:** User mentioned toggle concept from Reddit post.
**Why rejected:** Per-task only, doesn't protect brainstorm's research phase, overlaps with existing agent roles.
**DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Need a standalone research workflow separate from brainstorm.

#### 3. Hook-injected behavioral toggle (rejected)

**What:** Inject hallucination reduction rules via hooks, toggled on/off.
**Why explored:** Closest to the Reddit post's "research mode" concept.
**Why rejected:** Fragile state management (temp files between hook invocations), throttles creative output, against Cape's workflow architecture.
**DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Cape adopts a formal mode/state system.

### Scope boundaries

**In scope:** Agent instruction changes (internet-researcher, codebase-investigator), new fact-checker agent, brainstorm dispatch addition.
**Out of scope:** Hook changes, CLAUDE.md rules, command toggle, main conversation guardrails, execute-plan integration.

### Open questions
- None remaining (all resolved during brainstorm)

## Design discovery

### Key decisions made

| Question | Answer | Implication |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| Which approach? | Enhance agents + fact-checker | Changes to 2 existing agents, 1 new agent, brainstorm update |
| Fact-checker dispatch | Automatic from brainstorm | Runs on design summary at step 3 |
| Citation format | Inline with URL + tier | Citations travel with claims through the skill chain |
| Fact-checker in execute-plan? | No | Scoped to brainstorm only |

### Research deep-dives

#### Anthropic hallucination reduction docs
**Question:** What exact techniques does Anthropic recommend?
**Sources:** code.claude.com/docs — Reduce Hallucinations page
**Conclusion:** Three core techniques (uncertainty admission, citation verification, direct quote extraction) work as system prompt instructions. Advanced techniques (chain-of-thought, best-of-N) not needed for agent-level guards.

#### Agent context and conversation bloat
**Question:** Would citation requirements bloat the main conversation context?
**Sources:** Discussion during brainstorm
**Conclusion:** Agent output lives in agent context, not main conversation. Main Claude writes a concise summary. Citations stay in agent context — no bloat. Temp files unnecessary.

### Dead-end paths

#### Temp file for cited references
**Why explored:** User suggested storing citations in a temp file to reduce context bloat.
**What found:** Agent context boundary already solves this — agent output doesn't land in main conversation. Temp files add maintenance burden and break flow.
**Why abandoned:** Problem doesn't exist; agent context is naturally isolated.

### Open concerns raised
- Creative output tradeoff -> Resolved by guarding at agent layer only
- Citation bloat in conversation -> Resolved by agent context boundary
- Fact-checker slowing brainstorm -> Resolved by running once on design summary, not per-finding
